notes.heading: Introduction to Chemical Kinetics
notes.text: Chemical kinetics is the branch of chemistry that deals with the speed of chemical reactions and the factors affecting the speed. It explains how fast a reaction happens, why some reactions are fast or slow, and how the speed of a reaction can be increased or decreased. Thermodynamics tells whether a reaction is possible or not, while chemical kinetics tells how fast the reaction occurs.
points: Chemical kinetics is the study of the rate of chemical reactions and the mechanism by which reactions occur.
Fast reaction example: AgNO3 + NaCl → AgCl↓ + NaNO3. A white precipitate of silver chloride forms immediately.
Slow reaction example: 4Fe + 3O2 + xH2O → 2Fe2O3·xH2O. Rusting of iron takes a long time.
Moderately slow reaction example: CH3COOC2H5 + H2O → CH3COOH + C2H5OH. Hydrolysis of ester takes some time.
Important topics in chemical kinetics include rate of reaction, order of reaction, molecularity, rate law, effect of concentration, temperature and catalyst, half-life, and activation energy.
Example: Diamond can convert into graphite because graphite is more stable, but the process is extremely slow. Therefore, diamond remains diamond for a very long time.

notes.heading: Rate of a Chemical Reaction
notes.text: The rate of a chemical reaction is the speed at which reactants are converted into products. During a reaction, the concentration of reactants decreases and the concentration of products increases with time.
points: For a reaction A → B, A is the reactant and B is the product.
Rate = decrease in concentration of reactant / time taken.
Rate = increase in concentration of product / time taken.
Rate of disappearance of A = -Δ[A]/Δt.
Rate of appearance of B = +Δ[B]/Δt.
The negative sign is used for reactants because their concentration decreases with time, but rate is always taken as positive.
Unit of rate of reaction = mol L^-1 s^-1.
Example: If concentration of A changes from 0.50 mol L^-1 to 0.30 mol L^-1 in 10 s, then Δ[A] = 0.30 - 0.50 = -0.20 mol L^-1. Rate = -(-0.20)/10 = 0.02 mol L^-1 s^-1.
Rate of reaction is the change in concentration of reactants or products per unit time.

notes.heading: Average Rate and Instantaneous Rate
notes.text: The rate of reaction can be studied as average rate or instantaneous rate. Average rate is measured over a time interval, while instantaneous rate is measured at a particular instant of time.
points: Average rate is the rate of reaction during a given time interval.
For A → B, average rate = -Δ[A]/Δt or +Δ[B]/Δt.
Example: If concentration of A changes from 0.60 mol L^-1 to 0.40 mol L^-1 in 20 s, then Δ[A] = -0.20 mol L^-1 and average rate = -(-0.20)/20 = 0.01 mol L^-1 s^-1.
Instantaneous rate is the rate of reaction at a particular instant of time.
When Δt → 0, average rate becomes instantaneous rate.
For reactant A, instantaneous rate = -d[A]/dt.
For product B, instantaneous rate = +d[B]/dt.
Average rate uses Δ symbol, while instantaneous rate uses d symbol.
For most chemical reactions, rate is maximum at the beginning because concentration of reactants is high. As time passes, reactant concentration decreases and rate also decreases.

notes.heading: Rate of Reaction for Stoichiometric Reactions
notes.text: For reactions having stoichiometric coefficients, the rate of reaction is written by dividing the rate of disappearance or appearance of each substance by its coefficient in the balanced equation.
points: For a simple reaction A → B, Rate = -d[A]/dt = +d[B]/dt.
For a general reaction aA + bB → cC + dD, Rate = -(1/a)d[A]/dt = -(1/b)d[B]/dt = +(1/c)d[C]/dt = +(1/d)d[D]/dt.
We divide by stoichiometric coefficients because substances may be consumed or formed in different amounts.
Example: For 2HI → H2 + I2, Rate = -(1/2)d[HI]/dt = +d[H2]/dt = +d[I2]/dt.
Example: For N2 + 3H2 → 2NH3, Rate = -d[N2]/dt = -(1/3)d[H2]/dt = +(1/2)d[NH3]/dt.
Example: For 2N2O5 → 4NO2 + O2, Rate = -(1/2)d[N2O5]/dt = +(1/4)d[NO2]/dt = +d[O2]/dt.
Use negative sign for reactants because their concentration decreases.
Use positive sign for products because their concentration increases.

notes.heading: Factors Affecting Rate of Reaction
notes.text: The rate of a chemical reaction depends on several factors such as concentration of reactants, temperature, catalyst, nature of reactants, surface area, and light.
points: Concentration of reactants: Generally, when concentration increases, rate increases because particles collide more frequently.
Example: Zn + 2HCl → ZnCl2 + H2. Zinc reacts faster with concentrated HCl than with dilute HCl.
Temperature: Rate increases when temperature increases because particles move faster and collide with more energy.
For many reactions, rate becomes nearly double for every 10°C rise in temperature.
Food spoils faster in summer because chemical and biological processes occur faster at higher temperature.
Catalyst: A catalyst is a substance that changes the rate of reaction without itself being consumed.
Example: 2KClO3 --MnO2--> 2KCl + 3O2. MnO2 acts as a catalyst.
Nature of reactants: Ionic reactions are usually fast because ions react quickly in solution. Covalent reactions are usually slower because bonds must break and form.
Example of fast ionic reaction: Ag+ + Cl- → AgCl↓.
Surface area: For solid reactants, larger surface area increases the rate of reaction. Powdered substances react faster than large pieces.
Example: Powdered CaCO3 reacts faster with HCl than marble chips. CaCO3 + 2HCl → CaCl2 + CO2 + H2O.
Light: Some reactions are affected by light and are called photochemical reactions.
Example: 2AgCl --sunlight--> 2Ag + Cl2.
Main NCERT factors: concentration, temperature, catalyst, and nature of reactants.

notes.heading: Rate Law and Rate Constant
notes.text: Rate law, also called rate equation, expresses the rate of a reaction in terms of the concentration of reactants. It is determined experimentally.
points: For a general reaction aA + bB → products, the rate law may be written as Rate = k[A]^x[B]^y.
In Rate = k[A]^x[B]^y, [A] and [B] are concentrations, x and y are powers of concentration terms, and k is the rate constant.
The powers x and y are not always equal to the stoichiometric coefficients a and b. They are found experimentally.
Example: For 2NO + O2 → 2NO2, the experimentally determined rate law is Rate = k[NO]^2[O2].
Rate constant k is also called specific reaction rate.
If the concentration of each reactant is unity, then Rate = k.
Rate constant is equal to the rate of reaction when concentration of each reactant is unity.

Characteristics of rate constant: it is constant for a reaction at fixed temperature, changes with temperature, does not change with concentration, depends on the nature of reaction, and has different units for different orders of reaction.
Example: If Rate = k[A]^2 and concentration of A is doubled, Rate = k[2A]^2 = 4k[A]^2. Therefore, rate becomes four times.
Rate of reaction changes with concentration, but rate constant does not change with concentration at fixed temperature.

notes.heading: Order of Reaction
notes.text: Order of reaction tells how the rate of reaction depends on the concentration of reactants. It is obtained from the rate law and not directly from the balanced chemical equation.
points: For a reaction aA + bB → products with rate law Rate = k[A]^x[B]^y, order of reaction = x + y.
Order of reaction is the sum of powers of concentration terms in the rate law.
If Rate = k[A], order = 1. It is a first order reaction.
If Rate = k[A]^2, order = 2. It is a second order reaction.
If Rate = k[A][B], order = 1 + 1 = 2. It is a second order reaction.
If Rate = k[A]^2[B], order = 2 + 1 = 3. It is a third order reaction.
If Rate = k[A]^0, then Rate = k because [A]^0 = 1. This is a zero order reaction.
In zero order reaction, rate does not depend on the concentration of reactant.
Order can be fractional, for example Rate = k[A]^(1/2), order = 1/2.
Order may be zero, whole number, or fractional.
Order is determined experimentally and cannot be predicted simply from the balanced equation.
Difference from molecularity: Order is found from rate law and can be zero or fractional, while molecularity is found from reaction mechanism and is always a whole number.

notes.heading: Units of Rate Constant
notes.text: The unit of rate constant k depends on the order of reaction. For a rate law Rate = k[A]^n, k = Rate/[A]^n.
points: Unit of rate = mol L^-1 s^-1.
Unit of concentration = mol L^-1.
For zero order reaction, Rate = k[A]^0 = k. Unit of k = mol L^-1 s^-1.
For first order reaction, Rate = k[A]. k = Rate/[A]. Unit of k = s^-1.
For second order reaction, Rate = k[A]^2. k = Rate/[A]^2. Unit of k = L mol^-1 s^-1.
For third order reaction, Rate = k[A]^3. k = Rate/[A]^3. Unit of k = L^2 mol^-2 s^-1.
General formula for unit of rate constant for order n: Unit of k = (mol L^-1)^(1-n) s^-1.
Another general formula: Unit of k = L^(n-1) mol^(1-n) s^-1.
Only for first order reaction, the unit of rate constant is always s^-1 and does not depend on concentration unit.

notes.heading: Molecularity of Reaction
notes.text: Molecularity is the number of reacting species taking part in an elementary step of a reaction. Reacting species may be atoms, ions, or molecules.
points: A reaction that occurs in a single step is called an elementary reaction.
Example: NO + O3 → NO2 + O2. One molecule of NO reacts with one molecule of O3, so molecularity = 2. This is a bimolecular reaction.
Unimolecular reaction: only one reacting species is involved. Example: NH4NO2 → N2 + 2H2O. Molecularity = 1.
Bimolecular reaction: two reacting species are involved. Example: 2HI → H2 + I2. Molecularity = 2.
Another bimolecular example: NO + O3 → NO2 + O2.
Trimolecular reaction: three reacting species are involved. Example: 2NO + O2 → 2NO2. Molecularity = 3.
Trimolecular reactions are rare because three particles must collide at the same time.
Molecularity is always a whole number.
Molecularity cannot be zero or fractional.
Molecularity is found from the reaction mechanism.
Molecularity is used only for elementary reactions, not for complex overall reactions.
Molecularity usually has values 1, 2, or rarely 3.
Molecularity represents the actual number of reacting particles, so it cannot be 0, 1/2, or 1.5.
Order is the sum of powers in the rate law and can be zero or fractional. Molecularity is the number of reacting species in an elementary step and is always a positive whole number.

notes.heading: Integrated Rate Equation for Zero Order Reaction
notes.text: Integrated rate equations help calculate the concentration of reactants at different times. A zero order reaction is one whose rate does not depend on the concentration of reactant.
points: For A → Products, zero order rate law is Rate = k[A]^0.
Since [A]^0 = 1, Rate = k. This means rate is constant.
Differential rate equation for disappearance of A: Rate = -d[A]/dt.
For zero order reaction: -d[A]/dt = k.
Integrated rate equation: [A] = [A]0 - kt.
Here [A] is concentration at time t, [A]0 is initial concentration, k is rate constant, and t is time.
Rearranged form: kt = [A]0 - [A].
Rate constant formula: k = ([A]0 - [A])/t.
Graph of [A] versus t is a straight line with negative slope.
For [A] = [A]0 - kt, slope = -k and intercept = [A]0.
Example of zero order reaction: Decomposition of ammonia on platinum surface at high pressure, 2NH3 --Pt--> N2 + 3H2.
Unit of k for zero order reaction = mol L^-1 s^-1.
In zero order reaction, the reaction proceeds at a constant rate independent of concentration.

notes.heading: Half-life of Zero Order Reaction
notes.text: Half-life is the time required for the concentration of a reactant to become half of its initial concentration. It is represented by t1/2.
points: For A → Products, initial concentration is [A]0.
After half-life, concentration becomes [A]0/2.
Zero order integrated rate equation: [A] = [A]0 - kt.
At half-life, t = t1/2 and [A] = [A]0/2.
Substitution gives [A]0/2 = [A]0 - kt1/2.
Formula for half-life of zero order reaction: t1/2 = [A]0/(2k).
For zero order reaction, half-life is directly proportional to initial concentration: t1/2 ∝ [A]0.
If initial concentration is doubled, half-life also becomes doubled.
For zero order reaction, [A] versus time graph is a straight line decreasing with time.
Summary: Rate = k, integrated equation [A] = [A]0 - kt, half-life t1/2 = [A]0/(2k), and unit of k = mol L^-1 s^-1.

notes.heading: Integrated Rate Equation for First Order Reaction
notes.text: A first order reaction is one whose rate depends on the first power of concentration of one reactant.
points: For A → Products, first order rate law is Rate = k[A].
Differential rate equation: -d[A]/dt = k[A].
Integrated rate equation: ln[A] = ln[A]0 - kt.
Another form: kt = ln([A]0/[A]).
Rate constant formula using natural log: k = (1/t) ln([A]0/[A]).
Formula using common logarithm: k = (2.303/t) log([A]0/[A]).
Graph of ln[A] versus t is a straight line.
For ln[A] = ln[A]0 - kt, slope = -k and intercept = ln[A]0.
Using common log: log[A] = log[A]0 - (k/2.303)t.
For log[A] versus t graph, slope = -k/2.303.
Unit of k for first order reaction = s^-1.
In first order reaction, rate depends directly on concentration. If concentration is doubled, rate also doubles.

notes.heading: Half-life of First Order Reaction
notes.text: Half-life of a first order reaction is the time required for the reactant concentration to become half of its initial concentration. It is independent of initial concentration.
points: First order integrated rate equation: k = (2.303/t) log([A]0/[A]).
At half-life, t = t1/2 and [A] = [A]0/2.
Substitution gives k = (2.303/t1/2) log([A]0/([A]0/2)).
This becomes k = (2.303/t1/2) log 2.
Formula: t1/2 = 0.693/k.
For first order reaction, half-life does not depend on initial concentration.
Comparison: Zero order half-life t1/2 = [A]0/(2k), depends on initial concentration. First order half-life t1/2 = 0.693/k, does not depend on initial concentration.
For a first order reaction, after every half-life the concentration becomes half: [A]0 → [A]0/2 → [A]0/4 → [A]0/8.
Example: If initial concentration is 100, after one half-life it becomes 50, after two half-lives 25, and after three half-lives 12.5.

notes.heading: Pseudo First Order Reaction
notes.text: A pseudo first order reaction is actually a higher order reaction, but it behaves like a first order reaction because one reactant is present in large excess.
points: Consider A + B → Products with actual rate law Rate = k[A][B]. The actual order is 2.
If B is present in large excess, its concentration remains almost constant during the reaction.
Then [B] = constant and Rate = k[A][B] becomes Rate = k'[A], where k' = k[B].
The reaction behaves like a first order reaction, so it is called pseudo first order reaction.
Pseudo means false or apparent. The reaction is not truly first order but appears first order.
NCERT example: Hydrolysis of ethyl acetate, CH3COOC2H5 + H2O → CH3COOH + C2H5OH.
For ester hydrolysis, actual rate law may be Rate = k[CH3COOC2H5][H2O]. Since water is in large excess, Rate = k'[CH3COOC2H5].
Thus hydrolysis of ester is a pseudo first order reaction.
Another example: Inversion of cane sugar, C12H22O11 + H2O → C6H12O6 + C6H12O6.
In inversion of cane sugar, sucrose reacts with water to form glucose and fructose. Water is present in excess, so the reaction behaves like first order.
Important points: Actual order is higher, usually second order; apparent order is first order; reason is one reactant is in large excess; rate law becomes Rate = k'[A].

notes.heading: Temperature Dependence of Rate of Reaction
notes.text: The rate of a chemical reaction increases with increase in temperature because molecules move faster and collide with more energy.
points: For many chemical reactions, rate becomes nearly double for every 10°C rise in temperature.
At higher temperature, molecules get more kinetic energy.
At higher temperature, molecules collide more frequently.
More molecules have enough energy to react.
Effective collisions increase and reaction becomes faster.
Effective collision is a collision that leads to product formation.
A reaction occurs only when colliding molecules have energy equal to or greater than a minimum energy.
Activation energy is the minimum energy required by reacting molecules to form products. It is represented by Ea.
Activated complex, also called transition state, is a high-energy unstable intermediate formed during a reaction.
Reaction path: Reactants → Activated complex → Products.
Activation energy formula: Ea = Energy of activated complex - Energy of reactants.
Example analogy: A stone must be pushed over a hill to move to the other side. Similarly, reactant molecules must cross the energy barrier called activation energy.
Higher temperature allows more molecules to cross the activation energy barrier.

notes.heading: Arrhenius Equation
notes.text: Arrhenius equation gives the relationship between rate constant, temperature, and activation energy. It explains why the rate of reaction increases when temperature increases.
points: Arrhenius equation: k = Ae^(-Ea/RT).
In the equation, k is rate constant, A is Arrhenius factor or frequency factor, Ea is activation energy, R is gas constant, T is temperature in Kelvin, and e is a mathematical constant.
A is called frequency factor or pre-exponential factor. It represents the frequency of collisions between reacting molecules.
The term e^(-Ea/RT) represents the fraction of molecules having energy equal to or greater than activation energy.
Logarithmic form using natural log: ln k = ln A - Ea/RT.
Logarithmic form using common log: log k = log A - Ea/(2.303RT).
For graph of log k versus 1/T, equation is log k = log A - (Ea/2.303R)(1/T).
Graph of log k versus 1/T is a straight line.
Slope of log k versus 1/T graph = -Ea/(2.303R).
Activation energy from slope: Ea = -2.303R × slope.
When temperature increases, Ea/RT decreases, e^(-Ea/RT) increases, k increases, and rate increases.
Higher activation energy decreases k and makes the reaction slower.
Catalyst increases rate by lowering activation energy.
A depends on collision frequency and orientation.
A reaction with low activation energy is faster, while a reaction with high activation energy is slower.

notes.heading: Calculation of Activation Energy Using Arrhenius Equation
notes.text: Arrhenius equation can be used to calculate activation energy when rate constants are given at two different temperatures.
points: At temperature T1, rate constant is k1: k1 = Ae^(-Ea/RT1).
At temperature T2, rate constant is k2: k2 = Ae^(-Ea/RT2).
Two-temperature Arrhenius formula: log(k2/k1) = (Ea/2.303R)[(T2 - T1)/(T1T2)].
Activation energy formula: Ea = [2.303R log(k2/k1) × T1T2]/(T2 - T1).
R is usually taken as 8.314 J K^-1 mol^-1.
Activation energy is obtained in J mol^-1 when R = 8.314 J K^-1 mol^-1 is used.
To convert J mol^-1 to kJ mol^-1, divide by 1000.
Temperature must always be in Kelvin.
Conversion formula: T(K) = T(°C) + 273.
Example conversions: 27°C = 300 K and 37°C = 310 K.
Example problem: k1 = 2.0 × 10^-3 s^-1 at T1 = 300 K and k2 = 4.0 × 10^-3 s^-1 at T2 = 310 K.
Generally, if T2 > T1, then k2 > k1 for most reactions.

notes.heading: Effect of Catalyst on Rate of Reaction
notes.text: A catalyst is a substance that changes the rate of a chemical reaction without itself being consumed. A catalyst usually increases the rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy.
points: Definition: A catalyst changes the rate of reaction but remains chemically unchanged at the end of the reaction.
Example: 2KClO3 --MnO2--> 2KCl + 3O2. MnO2 is the catalyst.
A catalyst provides an alternative path for the reaction.
The catalysed path has lower activation energy.
Without catalyst, activation energy is high and fewer molecules have enough energy to react, so reaction is slow.
With catalyst, activation energy is lower and more molecules have enough energy to react, so reaction is faster.
A catalyst does not change the energy of reactants and products.
A catalyst does not change ΔH or the overall heat of reaction.
For catalysed reaction, Ea(catalysed) < Ea(uncatalysed).
According to Arrhenius equation k = Ae^(-Ea/RT), when a catalyst lowers Ea, e^(-Ea/RT) increases and k increases.
A catalyst increases the rate of both forward and backward reactions equally.
A catalyst helps the reaction reach equilibrium faster but does not change the equilibrium constant K.
Important effects: lowers activation energy, provides alternative path, does not change ΔH, does not change equilibrium constant, and decreases reaction time.

notes.heading: Collision Theory of Chemical Reactions
notes.text: Collision theory explains chemical reactions at the molecular level. According to this theory, a chemical reaction occurs only when reactant molecules collide effectively.
points: Reactant molecules are always moving.
When molecules collide, reaction may occur, but every collision does not form products.
Effective collisions are collisions that lead to product formation.
Two conditions for effective collision are sufficient energy and proper orientation.
Effective collision = sufficient energy + proper orientation.
Energy requirement: Reactant molecules must have energy equal to or greater than activation energy, E ≥ Ea.
If molecules do not have enough energy, they collide and separate without reaction.
Proper orientation means molecules must collide in the correct direction.
Even molecules with enough energy may fail to react if orientation is wrong.
Ineffective collision is a collision that does not produce products.
Reasons for ineffective collision: molecules have less energy than activation energy or molecules do not have proper orientation.
Collision frequency is the number of collisions taking place per second per unit volume. It is represented by Z.
More collision frequency generally means higher rate, but only effective collisions produce products.
Steric factor accounts for proper orientation of molecules. It is represented by P.
For a bimolecular reaction A + B → Products, rate according to collision theory can be written as Rate = PZAB e^(-Ea/RT).
In Rate = PZAB e^(-Ea/RT), P is steric factor, ZAB is collision frequency between A and B, Ea is activation energy, R is gas constant, and T is temperature.
Arrhenius equation is k = Ae^(-Ea/RT). In collision theory, A = PZ.
Thus k = PZ e^(-Ea/RT).
Rate constant depends on collision frequency, proper orientation, activation energy, and temperature.

notes.heading: Important Graphs of Chemical Kinetics
notes.text: Graphs in chemical kinetics help identify the order of reaction and calculate the rate constant k.
points: Zero order reaction equation: [A] = [A]0 - kt.
For zero order reaction, graph of [A] versus t is a straight line.
In zero order graph, slope = -k and intercept = [A]0.
For zero order reaction, [A] decreases uniformly with time.
First order reaction equation: ln[A] = ln[A]0 - kt.
For first order reaction, graph of ln[A] versus t is a straight line.
In ln[A] versus t graph, slope = -k and intercept = ln[A]0.
Using common log, log[A] = log[A]0 - (k/2.303)t.
For log[A] versus t graph, slope = -k/2.303.
From common log graph, k = -2.303 × slope.
Arrhenius graph equation: log k = log A - (Ea/2.303R)(1/T).
Graph of log k versus 1/T is a straight line.
Slope of Arrhenius graph = -Ea/(2.303R).
Activation energy from Arrhenius graph: Ea = -2.303R × slope.
For first order reaction, t1/2 = 0.693/k and half-life is independent of initial concentration.
For first order reaction, concentration changes as 100 → 50 → 25 → 12.5, and each step takes the same time.
For zero order reaction, t1/2 = [A]0/(2k), so half-life depends on initial concentration.
To identify order from graph: if [A] versus t is straight line, reaction is zero order.
To identify order from graph: if ln[A] versus t is straight line, reaction is first order.
To identify order from graph: if log[A] versus t is straight line, reaction is first order.

notes.heading: Basic Numericals in Chemical Kinetics
notes.text: Chemical kinetics numerical problems are mainly based on formulas for rate of reaction, zero order reaction, first order reaction, half-life, and Arrhenius equation.
points: Rate of reaction formula: Rate = -Δ[A]/Δt or Rate = Δ[P]/Δt.
Numerical 1: If concentration of A decreases from 0.50 mol L^-1 to 0.30 mol L^-1 in 20 s, then Δ[A] = -0.20 mol L^-1. Rate = -(-0.20)/20 = 0.01 mol L^-1 s^-1.
Zero order formula: [A] = [A]0 - kt.
Zero order rate constant formula: k = ([A]0 - [A])/t.
Numerical 2: For a zero order reaction, [A]0 = 0.80 mol L^-1, [A] = 0.40 mol L^-1 after 40 s. k = (0.80 - 0.40)/40 = 0.40/40 = 0.01 mol L^-1 s^-1.
Half-life of zero order reaction: t1/2 = [A]0/(2k).
Numerical 3: For a zero order reaction, [A]0 = 0.60 mol L^-1 and k = 0.03 mol L^-1 s^-1. t1/2 = 0.60/(2 × 0.03) = 0.60/0.06 = 10 s.
First order rate constant formula: k = (2.303/t) log([A]0/[A]).
Half-life of first order reaction: t1/2 = 0.693/k.
Numerical 5: For a first order reaction, k = 0.0231 s^-1. t1/2 = 0.693/0.0231 = 30 s.

Numerical 6: For a first order reaction, [A]0 = 1.0 mol L^-1, k = 0.693 s^-1, and t = 1 s. Using k = (2.303/t) log([A]0/[A]), 0.693 = 2.303 log(1.0/[A]). Therefore log(1.0/[A]) = 0.693/2.303 = 0.3010 = log 2. So 1.0/[A] = 2 and [A] = 0.50 mol L^-1.
Important formula summary: Rate = -Δ[A]/Δt.
Important formula summary: Zero order integrated equation [A] = [A]0 - kt.
Important formula summary: Zero order k = ([A]0 - [A])/t.
Important formula summary: Zero order half-life t1/2 = [A]0/(2k).
Important formula summary: First order k = (2.303/t) log([A]0/[A]).
Important formula summary: First order half-life t1/2 = 0.693/k.
Exam tip: If the question says zero order, use [A] = [A]0 - kt.
Exam tip: If the question says first order, use k = (2.303/t) log([A]0/[A]).
